narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 91.9% chance that you would not pass, tipping the scales against admission. This prediction stems from a close look at key aspects of your academic record and background, compared to patterns seen in students who successfully passed the bar. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1st decile, which is notably lower than the average 5.91st decile among those who passed—suggesting your later undergraduate performance raised concerns about sustaining the rigor needed for law school and the bar. Nearly as important was your Year 1 decile also at 1st, far below the 6.04 average for passers, indicating early academic challenges that the model sees as a persistent risk for bar success. Your LSAT score of 37 helped pull the prediction somewhat toward passing, as it's close to the 37.27 average of successful students, showing strength in that area. While your upper-middle family income aligns well with the majority of bar passers (around 45%), and your plan to attend full-time matches the norm for 93% of them, these didn't outweigh the early decile impacts. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.10, slightly under the 3.24 average for those who passed, played a supporting role but wasn't among the top drivers. Overall, your profile compares to accepted students by having solid test scores and background fits, but the low early deciles stand out as the biggest departures, signaling to the model a higher bar failure risk despite your full-time commitment. This isn't a judgment on your potential or drive—many with similar starts have improved dramatically. To move forward, consider retaking courses or pursuing a post-baccalaureate program to boost your decile rankings, while leveraging your LSAT strength through targeted bar prep resources. Reapplying after demonstrating grade improvements could shift the model's view, and we'd encourage exploring conditional admission programs that value growth trajectories like yours. You're clearly invested in this path, and with focused steps on academic performance, a stronger prediction is within reach.
